original_abstract: Abstract Minority populations are largely absent from clinical research trials. The neglect of these populations has become increasingly apparent, with escalating cancer burdens and chronic disease. The challenges to recruitment of minorities in the United States are multiple including trust or lack thereof. Keys to successful recruitment are responding to community issues, its history, beliefs, and its social and economic pressures. The strategy we have used in many low-income, sometimes remote, communities is to recruit staff from the same community and train them in the required basic research methods. They are the first line of communication. After our arrival in the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001, we applied these principles learned over years of global research, to studies of chronic diseases. Beginning in 2004, we recruited and trained a team of local women who enrolled in a cohort of over five thousand Mexican Americans from randomly selected households. This cohort is being followed, and the team has remained, acquiring not only advanced skills (ultrasound, FibroScan, retinal photos, measures of cognition, etc.) but capacity to derive key health information. Currently, we are participating in multiple funded studies, including an NIH clinical trial, liver disease, obesity, and diabetes using multiomics aimed at developing precision medicine approaches to chronic disease prevention and treatment.
humanized_abstract: Minority populations are not included in most clinical research trials and there is a growing burden of cancer and chronic diseases. The reasons for this lack of inclusion in the U.S. are multiple and multi-layered and include a lack of trust. However, understanding and respecting the concerns, needs, and culture of communities can help address many of these issues. Key to such an approach is taking into consideration the history, culture, social and economic issues of the community being asked to participate. For low income communities, remote or hard to reach communities, we have found including members of the community on our staff and providing them with training in the basics of research participation is crucial and that these community staff members who know the community and its culture, can be the first point of access to our studies. We first applied this approach to the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001 to studies of chronic diseases and since 2004 have been leading and conducting research on a cohort of 5,000 Mexican Americans comprised of individuals from randomly selected households, who were recruited and enrolled in partnership with a team of local women from the community. The cohort and team have been ongoing and over the years the team members have undergone advanced training to acquire skills that include, but are not limited to ultrasound, FibroScan, retinal photos, measures of cognition and other skills that can be derived directly into measures of health and have become the basis for multiple studies funded nationally and internationally. We are presently participating in an NIH clinical trial as well as studies of liver disease, obesity and diabetes using multi-omics to develop precision medicine methods for the prevention and treatment of chronic diseases.
